lineage.summary: The pinned repository XML is a version of U-M’s 2006 uncorrected OCR, created from a historically reported 484-image scan of Böddeker’s complete 1878 edition. The cataloged body is Böddeker’s exact pp. 95-300 selection of forty-five discontiguous Harley 2253 works, which he transcribed and edited directly from the manuscript while consulting ten modeled earlier editions item by item. Those printed intermediaries are lateral consulted/collated branches, not replacements for Böddeker’s direct Harley base. Three complete 1878 scan objects, the PIMS OCR, the ten prior editions’ complete digital carriers, a modern METS edition, and DIMEV v1 are cached as research controls. Current BL manuscript images are advertised but unavailable; U-M delivery is Cloudflare-challenged; Ker’s complete target-covering printed facsimile has only physical/catalog/commercial routes. Rights remain distinct for medieval wording (U.S. public domain, U.K. unresolved), every historical edition, each scan/OCR derivative, U-M encoding/images, Ker, METS, DIMEV, Brill, and the repository artifact.

catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is Karl Böddeker’s exact forty-five-text edited selection from British Library Harley MS 2253 as printed on pp. 95-300 of his 1878 volume and serialized in U-M/General Corpus afy7793. It includes eight political poems, fourteen secular lyrics, eighteen religious lyrics, the Debate between Body and Soul, Maximian, the Life of Saint Marina, the Harrowing of Hell, and the Proverbs of Hendyng, together with Böddeker’s interleaved German headnotes and apparatus. It is not the complete manuscript, not every Middle English text in Harley 2253, not the complete 1878 book, and not a single authored medieval work.

notes: The catalog boundary follows the exact forty-five-text editorial unit represented in the BODY, not the broadest title string, full print volume, or physical manuscript.
All forty-five historical items are verse; the whole unit is mixed because Böddeker’s prose headnotes and apparatus are substantial and retained.
Thematic print order must not be mistaken for manuscript order. Exact current folio and METS article locators are recorded item by item.

The XML profile declares German because the edition frame is German; direct inspection and modern controls establish Middle English as the historical texts’ primary language and Anglo-Norman in one bilingual item.
Böddeker’s circa-1310 dating and Leominster framing are not adopted wholesale; current BL/METS evidence is scoped to the witness component and preserves uncertainty.

Rights and access claims are source-specific in the companion lineage manifest; public-domain medieval wording or 1878 editorial matter does not automatically determine rights in later photographs, OCR, metadata, or editions.

summaries.value: An edited selection of forty-five anonymous Middle English verse texts from British Library Harley MS 2253, arranged by Karl Böddeker in 1878 into political, secular, religious, debate, hagiographic, dramatic, and proverbial groupings. The historical texts range from political songs and love lyrics to Marian devotion, Passion meditation, body-soul debate, saint’s life, verse drama, and wisdom literature. Böddeker’s substantial German prose headnotes and textual notes are part of the cataloged editorial unit but remain distinct from the medieval works.

The source body contains three thematic DIV1 sections holding 8, 14, and 18 DIV2 poems, followed by five standalone DIV1 works: Debate between Body and Soul, Maximian, the Life of Saint Marina, the Harrowing of Hell, and the Proverbs of Hendyng. The forty-five historical items contain 4,027 encoded L elements and 474 LG elements; 201 body P elements carry interleaved editorial prose.

The exact selected unit occupies Böddeker printed pp. 95-300. It excludes the German introduction and grammar (roman pp. i-xvi and Arabic pp. 1-92), the section half-title and blank pp. 93-94, the glossary (pp. 301-451), appendix (pp. 452-463), and correction page 464. The chosen texts occupy discontiguous Harley folios 55v-128v, and Böddeker’s political/secular/religious order differs substantially from manuscript order.

Böddeker worked directly from Harley 2253 but also compared many earlier editions and parallel texts, occasionally recording or adopting an earlier editor’s reading. The graph therefore treats Harley as the direct source witness and Böddeker as transcriber/editor/collator, without flattening every cited edition into one common upstream source. His discussion of the Digby 86 parallel was mediated by E. Stengel and discovered only after the selected text had already been printed; the comparison appendix is outside afy7793.

U-M describes the 2006 electronic text as uncorrected OCR with no content editing, encoded at TEI in Libraries Level 1 and linked historically to 484 page images. The repository XML retains the forty-five selected texts but represents excluded front and back matter mainly as page-break placeholders. Current automated access to the U-M resolver encounters a Cloudflare challenge, and no challenge page is cached as source evidence.
